psy-sec6-u1-L1-Q1.1: Heuristics are indeed mental shortcuts used for quick decisions, not careful analytical processes.

psy-sec6-u1-L1-Q2.0: Heuristics trade accuracy for speed, so they do not guarantee perfect answers.
psy-sec6-u1-L1-Q2.2: Heuristics speed up thinking rather than slowing it down.
psy-sec6-u1-L1-Q2.3: Heuristics actually introduce systematic errors rather than eliminating them.

psy-sec6-u1-L1-Q4.1: The person made a quick judgment based on crowd behavior, not by researching online.
psy-sec6-u1-L1-Q4.2: The choice was driven by the crowd signal, not random selection.
psy-sec6-u1-L1-Q4.3: The person chose a coffee shop, so they did not avoid both.

psy-sec6-u1-L1-Q5.1: Heuristics continue to operate even when they produce errors; they do not stop working.
psy-sec6-u1-L1-Q5.2: The brain does not automatically correct systematic errors from heuristics.
psy-sec6-u1-L1-Q5.3: Reflexes are involuntary physical responses, not products of heuristic errors.

psy-sec6-u1-L2-Q1.1: Evolutionary pressures favored fast decisions over perfectly accurate ones in dangerous environments.

psy-sec6-u1-L2-Q2.0: The brain does not move like a muscle; its energy use comes from neural processing.
psy-sec6-u1-L2-Q2.2: Memories are stored as neural connections, not physical objects.
psy-sec6-u1-L2-Q2.3: While the brain generates heat, that is a byproduct, not the reason for its high energy use.

psy-sec6-u1-L2-Q4.0: Modern brains are roughly the same size as ancient ones; size is not the issue.
psy-sec6-u1-L2-Q4.2: People still use heuristics constantly; they did not stop in 1900.
psy-sec6-u1-L2-Q4.3: Modern problems like financial decisions and social media are often more complex than ancient ones.

psy-sec6-u1-L2-Q5.1: Analyzing every single decision would be exhausting and impractical.
psy-sec6-u1-L2-Q5.2: Shortcuts do sometimes cause problems, so they are not always perfect.
psy-sec6-u1-L2-Q5.3: When to use shortcuts has nothing to do with the day of the week.

psy-sec6-u1-L2-Q6.0: The goal is to recognize when shortcuts fail, not to eliminate the useful mental tools we rely on daily.

psy-sec6-u1-L3-Q1.1: System 1 is defined by its fast, automatic nature; it is not slow or deliberate.

psy-sec6-u1-L3-Q3.0: System 1 often gives quick good-enough answers but is not always correct.
psy-sec6-u1-L3-Q3.2: System 2 exists in all people; it is activated for deliberate, effortful thinking.
psy-sec6-u1-L3-Q3.3: System 1 is an older, more primitive mode of thinking, not a recent invention.

psy-sec6-u1-L3-Q5.0: System 2 would involve comparing prices and ingredients, which the tired shopper did not do.
psy-sec6-u1-L3-Q5.2: Shopping involves decision-making, which always engages thinking systems.
psy-sec6-u1-L3-Q5.3: One system typically dominates at any given time; they do not operate equally simultaneously.

psy-sec6-u1-L3-Q6.0: System 2 is not needed for extremely simple tasks, so it would not fail on them.
psy-sec6-u1-L3-Q6.1: Being well-rested and focused is when System 2 works best, not when it fails.
psy-sec6-u1-L3-Q6.3: Using a calculator actually helps System 2 by offloading computation.

psy-sec6-u1-L4-Q1.0: Cognitive biases produce systematic, predictable errors in the same type of situation, not random ones.

psy-sec6-u1-L4-Q2.0: Predictable errors are easier to study precisely because they follow patterns.
psy-sec6-u1-L4-Q2.2: Cognitive biases affect everyone regardless of intelligence.
psy-sec6-u1-L4-Q2.3: Predictable errors are still errors; they are not secretly correct answers.

psy-sec6-u1-L4-Q4.0: Cognitive biases affect all types of decisions, not just emotional ones.
psy-sec6-u1-L4-Q4.1: Education helps you spot biases but does not make you immune to them.
psy-sec6-u1-L4-Q4.3: Cognitive biases are found across all cultures, not only Western ones.

psy-sec6-u1-L4-Q6.1: Anchoring bias involves being influenced by initial numbers, which is not what the manager is doing.
psy-sec6-u1-L4-Q6.2: Confirmation bias involves seeking supporting evidence, not claiming personal objectivity.
psy-sec6-u1-L4-Q6.3: The availability heuristic involves overestimating frequency of easily recalled events.

psy-sec6-u2-L1-Q1.1: Anchoring bias is well-documented: the first piece of information disproportionately shapes later judgments.

psy-sec6-u2-L1-Q2.1: Seeing a higher price first makes the lower price feel like a deal, not overpriced.
psy-sec6-u2-L1-Q2.2: The first car creates a reference point that changes how the second car feels.
psy-sec6-u2-L1-Q2.3: Anchoring means the first price sticks in memory and influences judgment of the next.

psy-sec6-u2-L1-Q3.0: Research shows anchoring works even with completely irrelevant numbers.
psy-sec6-u2-L1-Q3.2: Anchoring affects people of all mathematical abilities.
psy-sec6-u2-L1-Q3.3: Knowing about anchoring reduces but does not eliminate its effect.

psy-sec6-u2-L1-Q5.1: Setting the anchor first is a strength in negotiation, not a weakness.
psy-sec6-u2-L1-Q5.2: Anchoring research shows the first number significantly influences the final outcome.
psy-sec6-u2-L1-Q5.3: Anchoring applies to all types of numerical negotiation, including salaries.

psy-sec6-u2-L1-Q6.0: Even experts who understand anchoring bias are still influenced by anchors, just somewhat less so.

psy-sec6-u2-L2-Q1.0: They describe the exact same fact using different framing; the information content is identical.

psy-sec6-u2-L2-Q2.0: The negative frame of a 10% chance of death makes people less likely to choose surgery.
psy-sec6-u2-L2-Q2.2: Research consistently shows people respond very differently to gain vs. loss framing.
psy-sec6-u2-L2-Q2.3: Both statements influence decisions because the framing activates different emotional responses.

psy-sec6-u2-L2-Q4.0: While gain frames are appealing, loss aversion makes loss frames roughly twice as motivating.
psy-sec6-u2-L2-Q4.2: Research consistently shows loss frames have stronger effects than gain frames.
psy-sec6-u2-L2-Q4.3: Both types of frames influence behavior, but loss frames do so more powerfully.

psy-sec6-u2-L2-Q5.1: Both outlets can use accurate data while framing it differently; framing is not lying.
psy-sec6-u2-L2-Q5.2: This involves how the same data is presented, which is framing, not anchoring.
psy-sec6-u2-L2-Q5.3: Both headlines describe the same crime statistics from different angles.

psy-sec6-u2-L3-Q1.0: Loss aversion means the pain of losing $50 is about twice as strong as the pleasure of gaining $50.

psy-sec6-u2-L3-Q2.0: Decades of research show losses and gains are not felt equally.
psy-sec6-u2-L3-Q2.2: Gains feel weaker than losses, not stronger.
psy-sec6-u2-L3-Q2.3: The typical ratio is about 2:1, not 10:1.

psy-sec6-u2-L3-Q4.0: Gain framing is motivating but research shows it is weaker than loss framing.
psy-sec6-u2-L3-Q4.2: Studies consistently show loss framing produces measurably stronger behavioral effects.
psy-sec6-u2-L3-Q4.3: How a bonus is framed significantly changes employee motivation and performance.

psy-sec6-u2-L3-Q6.1: No initial price was stated, so there was no anchor to set expectations.
psy-sec6-u2-L3-Q6.2: The study did not involve people seeking confirming evidence about mugs.
psy-sec6-u2-L3-Q6.3: The mug was the same physical object for both groups, not described differently.

psy-sec6-u2-L4-Q1.0: Retailers often inflate the original price to create an anchor that makes the sale price seem like a great deal.

psy-sec6-u2-L4-Q2.0: The was-price rarely reflects actual manufacturing cost; it is a marketing anchor.
psy-sec6-u2-L4-Q2.2: A high original price tag does not prove quality; it just anchors your perception.
psy-sec6-u2-L4-Q2.3: This pricing technique predates modern inflation adjustments and is a deliberate anchoring strategy.

psy-sec6-u2-L4-Q3.0: Some people do buy the small; the decoy is the option designed to make another look better.
psy-sec6-u2-L4-Q3.2: The large is the target, not the decoy; the theater wants you to buy it.
psy-sec6-u2-L4-Q3.3: The pricing gap between medium and large is suspiciously small, which is a classic decoy setup.

psy-sec6-u2-L4-Q5.0: You cannot shop without seeing prices; the key is having your own reference point.
psy-sec6-u2-L4-Q5.2: Always buying the most expensive option means anchoring traps you at the top.
psy-sec6-u2-L4-Q5.3: Most stores use sale signs; avoiding them entirely is impractical.

psy-sec6-u3-L1-Q1.0: Confirmation bias is the opposite: people seek information that confirms, not contradicts, their beliefs.

psy-sec6-u3-L1-Q2.1: Carefully comparing all options would counteract confirmation bias, not exemplify it.
psy-sec6-u3-L1-Q2.2: Changing your mind based on evidence is rational thinking, not confirmation bias.
psy-sec6-u3-L1-Q2.3: Randomly selecting sources would avoid the selective bias, not demonstrate it.

psy-sec6-u3-L1-Q4.1: The manager is not actively searching for records; they are remembering selectively.
psy-sec6-u3-L1-Q4.2: Selective interpretation involves reading ambiguous data as supporting your view, not memory distortion.
psy-sec6-u3-L1-Q4.3: The manager\'s belief filters their memory, which is a textbook example of confirmation bias.

psy-sec6-u3-L1-Q5.0: Trusting your first instinct feeds confirmation bias rather than combating it.
psy-sec6-u3-L1-Q5.1: Avoiding all information is impractical and would not help make better decisions.
psy-sec6-u3-L1-Q5.3: Discussing only with people who agree creates an echo chamber that strengthens the bias.

psy-sec6-u3-L2-Q1.0: Social media algorithms prioritize engagement, which means showing you content you already agree with.

psy-sec6-u3-L2-Q2.0: Echo chambers limit viewpoints rather than exposing you to many different ones.
psy-sec6-u3-L2-Q2.2: Echo chambers remove critics, so you are not forced to defend your opinions.
psy-sec6-u3-L2-Q2.3: Echo chambers directly amplify confirmation bias by filtering out dissent.

psy-sec6-u3-L2-Q5.0: Echo chambers actually restrict information rather than providing too much.
psy-sec6-u3-L2-Q5.2: Echo chambers reinforce your existing views rather than forcing you to change them.
psy-sec6-u3-L2-Q5.3: Echo chambers affect rapid, emotional reactions rather than slowing down thinking.

psy-sec6-u3-L3-Q1.1: The availability heuristic is precisely about judging how common something is based on how easily examples come to mind.

psy-sec6-u3-L3-Q2.0: Plane crashes are extremely rare; they are far less common than car accidents.
psy-sec6-u3-L3-Q2.2: Most people do not have accurate aviation statistics; they rely on mental impressions.
psy-sec6-u3-L3-Q2.3: Flying is statistically much safer than driving per mile traveled.

psy-sec6-u3-L3-Q4.0: Heart disease is underestimated despite being the leading cause because it lacks dramatic news coverage.
psy-sec6-u3-L3-Q4.2: Cancer is common and affects many families, so it is not typically as overestimated as rare dramatic events.
psy-sec6-u3-L3-Q4.3: Car accidents are common enough that people have a more realistic sense of their frequency.

psy-sec6-u3-L3-Q6.1: Anchoring involves a specific number influencing estimates, not a documentary creating vivid memories.
psy-sec6-u3-L3-Q6.2: Confirmation bias involves seeking evidence for existing beliefs, not having risk inflated by a film.
psy-sec6-u3-L3-Q6.3: The framing effect involves how the same information is presented, not emotional recall from media.

psy-sec6-u3-L4-Q1.0: News overrepresents dramatic, unusual events and underrepresents common ones, distorting perceived frequency.

psy-sec6-u3-L4-Q2.0: News organizations seek engagement, not deliberate misinformation; the bias is a structural byproduct.
psy-sec6-u3-L4-Q2.2: News specifically covers unusual events, not everyday occurrences.
psy-sec6-u3-L4-Q2.3: Most people consume enough media for the availability effect to influence their risk perception.

psy-sec6-u3-L4-Q5.1: Kidnapping by strangers is extremely rare; the fear is driven by media coverage, not rational analysis.
psy-sec6-u3-L4-Q5.2: Not all news is fake; the issue is that coverage distorts perceived frequency of events.
psy-sec6-u3-L4-Q5.3: Children benefit from outdoor play; the extremely low risk does not justify total avoidance.

psy-sec6-u3-L4-Q6.0: An anchor rate is not a real statistical term; anchoring refers to initial reference numbers.
psy-sec6-u3-L4-Q6.2: Frame rate refers to video display speed, not event frequency in a population.
psy-sec6-u3-L4-Q6.3: Default rate is not a standard term for actual event frequency.

psy-sec6-u4-L1-Q2.0: Anchoring involves initial numbers influencing estimates, not reluctance to sell stocks.
psy-sec6-u4-L1-Q2.2: Confirmation bias involves seeking supporting evidence, not fear of accepting a loss.
psy-sec6-u4-L1-Q2.3: The availability heuristic involves overestimating frequency, not holding onto losing investments.

psy-sec6-u4-L1-Q5.0: The bias blind spot affects people of all ages, not only children.
psy-sec6-u4-L1-Q5.2: The bias blind spot is a cognitive pattern, not one that causes physical symptoms.
psy-sec6-u4-L1-Q5.3: The bias blind spot operates in individuals, not only in group settings.

psy-sec6-u4-L1-Q6.1: The framing effect alone does not explain the news-driven fear or the post-purchase review seeking.
psy-sec6-u4-L1-Q6.2: Loss aversion alone does not explain the news influence or review-seeking behavior.
psy-sec6-u4-L1-Q6.3: Multiple biases are clearly at work here, not zero.

psy-sec6-u4-L2-Q1.1: No initial number was given that would serve as an anchor.
psy-sec6-u4-L2-Q1.2: The framing effect involves how information is presented, not personal experience inflating estimates.
psy-sec6-u4-L2-Q1.3: The endowment effect involves overvaluing things you own, not overestimating disease frequency.

psy-sec6-u4-L2-Q2.0: Loss aversion involves fear of losses, not selective sharing of political articles.
psy-sec6-u4-L2-Q2.1: The decoy effect involves pricing strategies, not selective information sharing.
psy-sec6-u4-L2-Q2.3: The halo effect involves one positive trait coloring overall judgment, not selective evidence sharing.

psy-sec6-u4-L2-Q3.0: Confirmation bias involves seeking supporting evidence, not being influenced by displayed prices.
psy-sec6-u4-L2-Q3.1: The availability heuristic involves ease of recall, not price comparison in a store.
psy-sec6-u4-L2-Q3.3: Mean world syndrome involves overestimating danger from news, not furniture pricing.

psy-sec6-u4-L2-Q4.0: Who told you is less relevant than how it was framed for detecting framing effects.
psy-sec6-u4-L2-Q4.2: Asking about cost does not reveal whether framing is influencing your judgment.
psy-sec6-u4-L2-Q4.3: Whether friends agree does not help detect if presentation is warping your decision.

psy-sec6-u5-L1-Q1.1: The Dunning-Kruger effect is well-documented: people with low skill consistently overrate their ability.

psy-sec6-u5-L1-Q2.0: Dunning-Kruger is about genuine miscalibration, not deliberate deception.
psy-sec6-u5-L1-Q2.2: Beginners do not have more natural talent; they simply cannot see what they are missing.
psy-sec6-u5-L1-Q2.3: Beginners have studied less, not more, which is why they overestimate.

psy-sec6-u5-L1-Q3.0: The lowest-scoring students did not recognize their poor performance at all.
psy-sec6-u5-L1-Q3.2: All students in the study provided estimates; none refused.
psy-sec6-u5-L1-Q3.3: The lowest scorers were the least accurate in their self-assessment.

psy-sec6-u5-L1-Q6.1: One class is insufficient for accurate mental health diagnosis, which requires years of clinical training.
psy-sec6-u5-L1-Q6.2: One introductory class is nowhere near enough for accurate clinical diagnosis.
psy-sec6-u5-L1-Q6.3: Overconfidence after minimal training is a classic cognitive bias pattern.

psy-sec6-u5-L2-Q1.0: Research shows people claiming 99% certainty are wrong about 40% of the time, revealing severe overconfidence.

psy-sec6-u5-L2-Q2.0: By definition, most people cannot be above average; this is mathematically impossible.
psy-sec6-u5-L2-Q2.2: Driving test difficulty is irrelevant to the self-assessment bias shown here.
psy-sec6-u5-L2-Q2.3: People are the opposite of modest; they consistently overrate their driving ability.

psy-sec6-u5-L2-Q4.0: Planning too far ahead is not a recognized cognitive bias.
psy-sec6-u5-L2-Q4.2: Overcomplicating plans is a different issue from systematically underestimating time requirements.
psy-sec6-u5-L2-Q4.3: The planning fallacy is about poor time estimation, not about lack of follow-through.

psy-sec6-u5-L2-Q6.1: Trusting optimistic confidence over historical data is exactly how the planning fallacy causes problems.
psy-sec6-u5-L2-Q6.2: Splitting the difference still underestimates based on the pattern of past projects.
psy-sec6-u5-L2-Q6.3: Canceling entirely is an overreaction; using past data for realistic planning is the right approach.

psy-sec6-u5-L3-Q2.0: At the very start, people have not yet learned enough to feel the confidence drop.
psy-sec6-u5-L3-Q2.1: After learning a little is actually when overconfidence peaks, not when it drops.
psy-sec6-u5-L3-Q2.3: True experts have rebuilt their confidence on a solid foundation of real knowledge.

psy-sec6-u5-L3-Q3.0: Feeling less confident after learning more is a sign of growth, not decline, because you now see complexity you previously missed.

psy-sec6-u5-L3-Q4.0: Someone at the peak of overconfidence would feel supremely confident, not humbled by complexity.
psy-sec6-u5-L3-Q4.2: A complete beginner would not yet know enough to recognize their own knowledge gaps.
psy-sec6-u5-L3-Q4.3: The plateau of sustainability involves confident, calibrated expertise, not feelings of inadequacy.

psy-sec6-u5-L4-Q1.0: Imposter syndrome involves underestimating your abilities and feeling like a fraud despite genuine competence.

psy-sec6-u5-L4-Q2.0: They are opposite phenomena, not the same one.
psy-sec6-u5-L4-Q2.2: Imposter syndrome typically affects competent people, not beginners.
psy-sec6-u5-L4-Q2.3: The Dunning-Kruger effect is most pronounced in beginners, not experts.

psy-sec6-u5-L4-Q4.0: People who are genuinely underqualified typically do not experience imposter syndrome; they may show overconfidence.
psy-sec6-u5-L4-Q4.2: People who avoid new challenges rarely encounter the situations that trigger imposter syndrome.
psy-sec6-u5-L4-Q4.3: Ambition-driven comparison to high standards is what fuels imposter syndrome.

psy-sec6-u5-L4-Q6.1: The feeling of being a fraud cannot be solved by more studying; it requires reframing, not more work.
psy-sec6-u5-L4-Q6.2: Fifteen years of engineering experience is strong evidence of genuine qualification.
psy-sec6-u5-L4-Q6.3: Ignoring the feeling does not address the underlying imposter syndrome pattern.
